questions.text: It seldom _ in Feburary
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "rains" is the correct simple present tense verb form to describe the general, habitual lack of rain in February.
"none of these" is not a valid answer choice.
"rained" is the simple past tense, which suggests a specific past rain event rather than a general statement.
"raining" is the present participle, which does not work in this context to convey the lack of rain.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option:Lions, like any other carnivores, _ on meat.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In this sentence, the subject “lions” is plural, referring to more than one lion. Therefore, we need to use the plural verb form “live” to agree with the subject.
“Lives” is incorrect because it uses the singular verb form which does not agree with the plural subject.
“Does live” is incorrect because it uses the singular verb “does” along with the base form of the verb “live”. This structure is not necessary or appropriate in this context.
“Living” is incorrect because it uses the present participle form of the verb “live”. In this sentence, we need a finite verb form to match the subject “lions”.

questions.explanation: Since the noun is plural, hence we will use a singular verb: live.

questions.text: Either my father or my brothers _ going to sell the car.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: ‘Is’ is a singular verb. Hence, this option is incorrect.
‘Are’ is a plural verb and is the most suitable option. Hence, this option is correct.
Although ‘were’ is a plural verb, we will not use it because we prefer to use the present tense when possible.
‘Was’ is a singular verb. Hence, it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: If a sentence has two subjects connected by or/nor, either/or, or neither/nor, the verb must agree with the later subject (which is, in this case, ‘brothers’). As the subject is plural, the verb must also be plural

questions.text: Everything _ fine when it's done correctly.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: 'Work' doesn't make grammatical sense. "Work" is not correct because it is not in the correct form. It is the base form of the verb, and it does not agree with the subject.
'Worked' is past tense
'will work’ is future tense and hence incorrect.

The sentence is in the present tense and needs a verb in the third person singular form to agree with the subject "everything’. "Works" is the correct form of the verb "work" in the present tense third person singular.

questions.explanation: ‘Worked’ is past tense and ‘will work' is future tense hence incorrect. ‘Work’ doesn't make grammatical sense. ‘Work’ implies all of it, and ‘works' implies that one has some particular pieces in mind.

questions.text: Would you mind _ (open) the window please, it's hot here.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Open" is the base form of the verb, but it does not match the grammatical structure of the sentence.
"Opens" is the third-person singular form of the verb, which is not appropriate here since the subject is "you."
"Opened" is the past tense form of the verb, which is not suitable in this context as the speaker is requesting an action in the present.
Opening' is a continuous present verb, hence correct.

questions.explanation: The sentence is in present tense and the action required is a continuous present. ‘Opened 'is a verb in the past tense, ‘open 'is in the present tense, and ‘opens' refers to a change in position. ‘Opening' is a continuous present verb, hence correct.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: It was so quiet you _ (can) hear a pin drop.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) can: The option "can" is in the present tense, but the sentence is describing a past situation ("It was so quiet"). The correct form for the past tense of "can" is "could." So, this option is not correct.

b) could: The option "could" is in the past tense and matches the description of the situation being in the past ("It was so quiet"). "Could" is the correct past tense form of "can," and it indicates the ability to hear a pin drop in the quiet environment. This option correctly completes the sentence.

c) shall: The option "shall" is not the appropriate word to complete the sentence. "Shall" is a modal verb used to express future possibility or intention, but it does not fit in the context of the sentence describing a past situation.

d) will: The option "will" is also not the correct choice for completing the sentence. "Will" is a modal verb used to express future actions or certainty, which does not fit the context of describing a past quiet moment.

questions.explanation: As the sentence is in the past tense (the word “was so quiet” was used), the missing word has to be in the past tense. Hence ‘could’ is most suitable as it is also grammatically correct when said out loud.

Is Doing cannot be used as to tell about a daily routine about a routine work we use present tense not continuous
Does is use to express hobby or a routine work So this is correct answer

questions.explanation: We use 'does' and with third-person singular pronouns (he, she, it) and with singular noun forms.We use 'do' and are with other personal pronouns (you, we, they) and with plural noun forms.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure.Have you _ the examination?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Take" is the infinitive form of the verb "to take", meaning to engage in an action or to carry out a task. In this sentence, "Have you take the examination?" is the not grammatically correct sentence in English
"Took" is the past simple form of the verb "to take". In this sentence, "Have you took the examination?" is grammatically incorrect.
The past participle (taken) is used to show a completed action before another, both occurring in the past.
Taking is the present participle form of the verb "to take". In this sentence, "Have you taking the examination?" is a grammatically incorrect sentence.

questions.explanation: the past participle (taken) is used to show a completed action before another, both occurring in the past.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure:I _ a strange noise.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Option A is correct because it uses the simple present tense, which is suitable for a general or habitual action. The sentence indicates a current occurrence of hearing a strange noise.
"Am hearing" is incorrect because the verb "hear" is not used in the present continuous tense in this sentence. The correct form is the simple present tense, which is "hear."
"Have been hearing" is incorrect because it implies a continuous action that started in the past and is still ongoing, which doesn't fit the context of a single occurrence of hearing a strange noise.
"Hears" is incorrect because it is the third person singular form, which does not match with the first person pronoun "I." The correct form for the first person is "hear."

questions.explanation: A. "Hear" is correct because it uses the simple present tense, which is suitable for a general or habitual action. The sentence indicates a current occurrence of hearing a strange noise.

questions.text: Choose the correct option:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Though he had awakened before the birds began to twitter, he laid in bed until long after the sun had arisen.
options.explanation: Incorrect because "laid" is the past tense of "lay" (which means to place something down), not the intransitive verb "lie" (which means to recline). The correct past tense of "lie" is "lay."

options.text: Though he had awakened before the birds began to twitter, he had laid in bed until long after the sun had arisen.
options.explanation: Incorrect because it uses "had laid," which is the past perfect form of "lay" (to place something down). The sentence requires the past tense of "lie," which is "lay."

options.text: Though he had awakened before the birds began to twitter, he lay in bed until long after the sun had arisen.
options.explanation: Correct because it uses the past tense "lay" of the intransitive verb "lie”.This is appropriate for the sentence's context, which describes a state of reclining in the past.

options.text: Though he had awakened before the birds began to twitter, he has lain in bed until long after the sun had arisen.
options.explanation: Incorrect because "has lain" is the present perfect tense, which does not fit the past perfect context set by "had awakened." The correct form for past events is "lay."

questions.explanation: Correct because it uses the past tense "lay" of the intransitive verb "lie”.This is appropriate for the sentence's context, which describes a state of reclining in the past.

questions.text: Choose the correct option:
questions.subject: English

options.text: One of the cornerstone of modern physics, the principle of the conservation of energy was formulated by Leibniz in the seventeenth century.
options.explanation: Incorrect because "cornerstone" should be pluralized as "cornerstones" to match "one of the."

options.text: One of the cornerstones of modern physics, the principal of the conservation of energy were formulated by Leibniz in the seventeenth century.
options.explanation: Incorrect for two reasons: "principal" is a homophone of "principle" but does not mean the same thing; "principal" means a person of highest authority, whereas "principle" refers to a fundamental truth. Additionally, "were formulated" is incorrect because "the principle of the conservation of energy" is singular and requires "was formulated."

options.text: One of the cornerstones of modern physics, the principle of the conservation of energy was formulated by Leibniz in the seventeenth century.
options.explanation: Correct because it uses the correct plural form "cornerstones" and the correct term "principle." The verb "was formulated" correctly agrees with the singular subject "the principle of the conservation of energy."

options.text: One of the cornerstones of modern physics, the principal of the conservation of energy was formulated by Leibniz in the seventeenth century.
options.explanation: Incorrect because it uses "principal" instead of "principle."

questions.explanation: Correct because it uses the correct plural form "cornerstones" and the correct term "principle." The verb "was formulated" correctly agrees with the singular subject "the principle of the conservation of energy."

questions.text: She — live in New York in the first half of 1990.

"Used to" is the correct verb form to use when describing a past habit or a state that existed in the past, but does not exist now. In this case, the sentence is saying that she lived in New York in the past, but does not live there now.
Indicates ability or possibility, which doesn't fit the context of describing past habitual actions.
Indicates necessity or obligation, which doesn't fit the context of describing past habitual actions.

questions.explanation: "Used to" is the correct verb form to use when describing a past habit or a state that existed in the past, but does not exist now. In this case, the sentence is saying that she lived in New York in the past, but does not live there now.

questions.text: Choose the correct option
questions.subject: English

options.text: The teacher made all the students rewrite their papers, because the first drafts were not acceptable.
options.explanation: This sentence is correct. "Made" is correctly followed by the base form of the verb "rewrite." "Were" is the correct verb to agree with the plural subject "drafts."

options.text: The teacher made all the students to rewrite their papers, because the first drafts were not acceptable.
options.explanation: This sentence is incorrect because "made" is followed by the base form of the verb without "to." It should be "made all the students rewrite."

options.text: The teacher made all the students rewrite their papers, because the first drafts was not acceptable.
options.explanation: This sentence is incorrect because "was" does not agree with the plural subject "drafts." It should be "were."

options.text: The teacher made all the students rewrite their papers, because first drafts were not acceptable.
options.explanation: This sentence is almost correct but is missing the article "the" before "first drafts." It should be "the first drafts."

questions.explanation: This sentence is correct. "Made" is correctly followed by the base form of the verb "rewrite." "Were" is the correct verb to agree with the plural subject "drafts."

questions.text: Spot the errorI do not need spent a lot of time reading drama to understand the conflict among the characters.

options.explanation: The verb "need" is correctly used here to indicate lack of necessity.

This is the past tense of "spend," but it's incorrect in this context. After "need," the correct form is the base form of the verb "spend," not the past tense. The correct sentence should be: "I do not need to spend a lot of time reading drama to understand the conflict among the characters."
This gerund is correctly used to describe the activity of reading drama.
This infinitive is correctly used to express the purpose or reason for reading drama.

The correct sentence should be: "I do not need to spend a lot of time reading drama to understand the conflict among the characters."

questions.text: Choose the correct option
questions.subject: English

options.text: The padding helps prevent soreness and let you spend more time on the saddle
options.explanation: This sentence has a subject-verb agreement error. "Let" should be "lets" to agree with the singular subject "padding."

options.text: The padding helps to prevent soreness and lets you spend more time on the saddle
options.explanation: This sentence is grammatically correct. "Lets" is the correct third-person singular form of the verb "let," and "to prevent soreness" is correctly used with "helps to."

options.text: The padding helps prevent soreness and let you spend more time on the saddle
options.explanation: This option has the same subject-verb agreement error as option A. "Let" should be "lets" to agree with the singular subject "padding."

options.text: The padding helps prevent soreness and lets you to spend more time on the saddle
options.explanation: This sentence has an unnecessary "to" before "spend." The correct structure after "lets" is the base form of the verb ("spend") without "to."

questions.explanation: This sentence is grammatically correct. "Lets" is the correct third-person singular form of the verb "let," and "to prevent soreness" is correctly used with "helps to."

questions.text: Choose the correct option
questions.subject: English

options.text: Then I'd better go make up for the bedroom. (the compensate for something; to replace something lost)
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. "Make up for" means to compensate for something, but the phrase is not used correctly here in relation to the bedroom.

options.text: Then I'd better go make for the bedroom. (to move towards something or a place)
options.explanation: This option is correct. "Make for" means to move towards something or a place, which fits the context of heading towards the bedroom.

options.text: Then I'd better go make up the bedroom. (To prepare a bed (or something) for use)
options.explanation: This option is also correct. "Make up the bedroom" means to prepare the bedroom for use, such as by arranging the bed or cleaning up.

options.text: Then I'd better go make out to be the bedroom. (to claim; to assert)
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. "Make out to be" means to claim or assert something, but it doesn't fit the context of going to the bedroom.

questions.explanation: This option is correct. "Make for" means to move toward something or a place, which fits the context of heading toward the bedroom.
